prompt: Recommend three works of fiction that you think every thoughtful adult should read at some point. Explain each in two or three sentences.
response:
1. Mrs. Dalloway by Virginia Woolf  
A single June day in post-WWI London becomes a prism for one woman’s entire life and for a shell-shocked veteran’s final hours. Woolf’s streaming sentences show how public time (clocks, parties, politics) and private memory interleave, forcing you to notice the invisible wars people carry inside ordinary hours.

2. The Left Hand of Darkness by Ursula K. Le Guin  
On a planet where the ambisexual Gethenians can be either male or female during their monthly cycle, a human envoy must learn that loyalty, love, and betrayal look nothing like the versions he was taught. The novel is a master-class in how speculative fiction can estrange your defaults—about gender, nation, and even the weather—so you return to Earth seeing your own society as the exotic one.

3. Chronicle of a Death Foretold by Gabriel García Márquez  
An entire Andean town knows a murder is going to happen, yet no one stops it; the book reads like a forensic prayer repeated until the ritual becomes confession. In under 120 pages García Márquez turns collective inaction into a mirror every community—and every reader—must eventually face.
